summary: Demand evidence agents completed and PRD readiness passed.

demand_status.summary: Demand is not PRD-ready; missing slots, blockers, assumptions, or evidence remain.

triage.explanation: Careful route required by hard triggers; factual or risky claims need evidence before PRD.

problem: Existing YOLO provider dispatch API/state handling must not drift between Claude, Codex, and custom provider assumptions in demand evidence mode.
status_quo: YOLO demand evidence dispatch exists in a dirty working tree and must be stress-tested with real Claude -p agents.
desired_outcome: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
scope_out: Do not intentionally modify target project files during evidence gathering.
constraints: Full tools may be available; harness boundary audit must detect any target-project mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.
risks: A provider-specific assumption can silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls.

protocol.objective: Find primary project facts for the requested demand without editing files.
protocol.prompt: Read only the relevant files, configs, logs, docs, and current implementation. Return claims with exact paths/lines or source summaries. Separate assumptions from facts.

evidence_tasks.reason: Factual project claims need primary evidence.

protocol.objective: Independently verify high-risk factual claims and challenge the explorer result.
protocol.prompt: Use independent reads or sources. Confirm, weaken, or contradict each claim. Any conflict must become a blocker instead of being averaged away.
protocol.required_when: evidence_policy === cross_check or claim risk is high

protocol.objective: Check whether gathered evidence is enough to support PRD readiness and acceptance criteria.
protocol.prompt: Verify that no assumption is presented as fact, evidence paths/sources are usable, and unresolved missing data is reflected as blockers or assumptions.

evidence_tasks.reason: Verify blockers and assumptions are not promoted to facts.

state.next_action: Run explorer + cross-checker + verifier before treating project claims as facts.

agent_results.claim: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: execution_policy declares project_mutation forbidden and restricts allowed_write_roots to evidence dispatch output directory only
evidence.why: This is the core contract that preserves tool capability while preventing target project mutation.

evidence.summary: Runtime builds before/after boundary snapshots using buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots, then converts any changes to readiness blockers
evidence.why: This is the enforcement mechanism that detects violations even when agents have full tools.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function normalizes provider selection and model configuration, preventing cross-provider leakage
evidence.why: Lines 437-449 and 450-463 show explicit provider-specific overrides that prevent Claude settings from leaking into Codex or custom providers.

evidence.summary: Claude provider explicitly clears claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, and claude_disallowed_tools to empty strings, relying on boundary enforcement instead of tool restriction
evidence.why: This confirms the doctrine claim that safety comes from prompt contract and boundary snapshots, not from tool flag restrictions.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation constructs provider-specific command line arguments without mixing provider settings
evidence.why: Each provider branch (codex/custom/claude) handles only its own configuration namespace.

evidence.summary: Test proves codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model and preserves independent sandbox configuration
evidence.why: This is evidence that cross-provider model leakage is prevented at the adapter level.

evidence.summary: Test confirms claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on boundary enforcement
evidence.why: This validates that full tool capability is preserved while safety comes from the boundary layer.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation even when agent results pass
evidence.why: This is evidence that the boundary enforcement works independently of agent output quality.

evidence.summary: Test confirms git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations
evidence.why: This shows the boundary detection works for files that git itself would track.

evidence.summary: Doctrine states Claude provider dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on prompt contract, artifact root, and boundary snapshots for safety
evidence.why: This is the product-level documentation that explains the design intent behind the implementation.

assumptions: The harness boundary detection works for both tracked and untracked project files in git repositories
Git ls-files command succeeds in git-initialized project roots during boundary snapshot
Provider agents respect the prompt contract even when they have full tool capability
risks: If boundary snapshot fails silently, agents could mutate project files without detection
If provider ignores prompt contract and has filesystem write tools, mutations could occur before boundary check
Non-git projects may have weaker boundary detection if walkFiles misses changes in excluded directories

result.notes: All three files in scope_in exist and show coherent implementation. Provider configuration preserves tool capability by design (clearing tool restrictions for Claude, not passing unnecessary flags to Codex/custom), and cross-provider leakage is prevented by provider-specific config namespaces and explicit overrides in executionConfig. The boundary enforcement layer (snapshots + diff + blockers) provides safety independent of tool configuration.

agent_results.claim: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: execution_policy declares project_mutation forbidden and restricts allowed_write_roots to evidence dispatch output directory only
evidence.why: This is the core contract that defines the safety boundary: agents can only write to the evidence artifact directory, not to target project files.

evidence.summary: Runtime builds before/after boundary snapshots using buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots, then converts any changes to readiness blockers
evidence.why: This is the enforcement mechanism that detects violations even when agents have full tools. Lines 566-572 show boundary changes become blockers.

evidence.summary: executionConfig function has provider-specific branches for codex and claude that prevent cross-provider leakage
evidence.why: Lines 437-449 clear claude model from codex provider, and lines 450-463 apply claude-specific config only when provider is claude. No cross-contamination occurs.

evidence.summary: Claude provider explicitly clears claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, and claude_disallowed_tools to empty strings
evidence.why: This confirms the design claim that Claude agents receive full tool capability by default, with safety coming from boundary enforcement rather than tool restrictions.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation has separate branches for codex, custom, and claude providers that never mix configuration namespaces
evidence.why: Each provider branch reads only its own configuration keys (codex_model, custom_command, claude_tools) and builds provider-specific args. No cross-provider leakage.

evidence.summary: Test proves codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model: asserts config.ai.provider is codex, config.ai.model is not claude, and codex sandbox settings are independent
evidence.why: Lines 211-215 validate that when provider is codex, the claude model setting is cleared and codex defaults are preserved.

evidence.summary: Test confirms claude dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags: asserts claude_tools, claude_allowed_tools, claude_disallowed_tools are all empty strings
evidence.why: Lines 248-253 validate that claude provider runs without tool restrictions, confirming full tool capability is preserved.

evidence.summary: Test verifies boundary blocks project file mutation: agent writes a file but boundary detects it and status becomes blocked with BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blocker
evidence.why: Lines 395-410 show that even when agent succeeds and returns valid result, any project mutation triggers a boundary violation blocker.

evidence.summary: Test confirms git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations: creates a git repo, agent writes untracked file, boundary detects the added file
evidence.why: Lines 432-448 validate that the boundary detection works for both tracked and untracked files in git repositories.

evidence.summary: Doctrine states Claude provider dispatch does not pass tool restriction flags and relies on prompt contract, artifact root, and boundary snapshots for safety
evidence.why: This is the product-level documentation that explains the design intent: safety comes from boundary enforcement, not from restricting agent tool access.

assumptions: Git ls-files command succeeds in git-initialized project roots during boundary snapshot
Non-git projects use walkFiles which covers the project tree but may miss changes in deeply nested excluded directories
Provider agents respect the prompt contract even when they have full tool capability
risks: If boundary snapshot fails silently, agents could mutate project files without detection
If provider ignores prompt contract and has filesystem write tools, mutations could occur before boundary check runs
Non-git projects may have weaker boundary detection if walkFiles misses changes due to filesystem race conditions or permission errors

result.notes: All explorer evidence claims are independently verified. The implementation has three distinct safety layers: (1) execution_policy contract forbids project mutation and restricts writes to evidence artifact root only, (2) provider-specific configuration branches in executionConfig and buildProviderInvocation prevent cross-provider model/tool leakage, (3) boundary snapshots before/after agent execution detect any violations and convert them to readiness blockers. Tests validate each layer independently. The doctrine documentation confirms this is an intentional design choice: Claude agents receive full tool capability by default, with safety provided by prompt contract and boundary enforcement rather than tool restriction flags.

agent_results.claim: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary and avoids cross-provider model leakage.

evidence.summary: All 10 evidence records from explorer and cross-checker have valid scope field (project), valid source fields (project_code, project_test, project_docs), and include all required fields: path, line, scope, source, summary, why
evidence.why: This confirms evidence structure compliance with the yolo.demand.evidence_result.v1 schema.

evidence.summary: Explorer and cross-checker both independently verified the same code sections for execution_policy, executionConfig provider isolation, and boundary snapshot enforcement
evidence.why: Independent verification of the same code sections by two agents confirms the evidence is reproducible and not dependent on single-agent interpretation.

evidence.summary: Explorer and cross-checker both verified test coverage for provider isolation, tool capability preservation, and boundary enforcement
evidence.why: Test evidence confirms the claims are validated by automated tests, not just code inspection.

evidence.summary: Assumptions are properly separated from facts in both agent results: git ls-files reliability, non-git walkFiles coverage, and provider agent contract compliance are all labeled as assumptions, not presented as evidence
evidence.why: This confirms that unverified claims are not promoted to facts. The assumptions acknowledge boundary conditions that cannot be verified from code/docs alone.

evidence.summary: Both explorer and cross-checker have missing: [] (empty array), confirming no verification gaps were identified
evidence.why: Empty missing array is required when nothing is missing. Both agents correctly followed this protocol requirement.

evidence.summary: Cross-checker independently verified all 10 explorer evidence claims without finding any conflicts or weakening any claim
evidence.why: Cross-check agreement status is pass with zero conflicts, meaning no claims were contradicted or found to be assumptions presented as facts.

evidence.summary: Provider adapter code was independently verified by both agents as having separate branches that never mix configuration namespaces
evidence.why: This is the implementation-level evidence that prevents cross-provider leakage at the argument construction stage.

evidence.summary: Doctrine documentation was independently verified by both agents as confirming the design intent for full tool capability with boundary-based safety
evidence.why: Documentation evidence confirms the implementation matches the documented design intent.

assumptions: The evidence paths and line numbers cited by explorer and cross-checker are accurate reflections of the actual codebase
The test files actually pass when run, not just that the test assertions exist
Git commands in boundary snapshot code succeed in practice, not just in theory
risks: If test suite is not run or does not pass, the test evidence may be misleading despite correct assertions
If boundary snapshot code has runtime errors not visible in static inspection, the enforcement may fail silently
If provider adapter has edge cases not covered by the inspected branches, cross-provider leakage could still occur

result.notes: Verification protocol confirms: (1) All 20 evidence records (10 from explorer, 10 from cross-checker) have proper structure with valid scope, source, path, line, summary, and why fields; (2) Assumptions are properly separated from facts in both agent results and acknowledge boundary conditions; (3) Both agents have missing: [] confirming no identified verification gaps; (4) Cross-checker independently verified all explorer claims with zero conflicts; (5) Evidence spans code, tests, and docs providing multi-layer validation. The PRD acceptance criteria is supported by verified evidence from implementation, automated tests, and documentation.
